anchored_threads.thread_name: The sthita-prajña / one whose insight is steady (verses 2.54-2.72) — closes here
anchored_threads.anchor_evidence: The engineering layer explicitly identifies BG 2.72 as the chapter's CLOSING verse: 'eṣā ('this') refers back over the chapter's whole argument from 2.11 to 2.71'. The verse names the terminal completion of the operator-state the chapter has been building since 2.11. The 'twenty-five years in' framing is the engineering correlate of the verse's anta-kāle 'pi force — the closing scenes of a career as the operational analog of the closing scenes of life. The chapter's third thread (the seasoned operator's state) closes here with the chapter-thesis-support persona's special charge to identify this verse as the destination.

progression_issue.explanation: The verse sits at position 2.72, the chapter's terminal verse. The engineering layer correctly identifies this position and its function: 'The chapter has argued toward an operator-state since 2.11; this verse names its terminal completion'. The slight register-shift to scope-honest-reflective is appropriate to the closing-verse position and does not break voice consistency.

verdict_reason: BG 2.72 is the chapter's CLOSING POINT. The engineering layer correctly identifies this and treats it with maximum scope-honesty (STRETCHED tag, MEDIUM confidence, explicit operational scoping). The chapter's hard edges are preserved: the steady mind is a real state (the operational rung is reachable, falsifiably so via the career-state journal) but is not flattened into engineering tenure (the falsifiability clause closes that misread). The chapter's metaphysical reach is explicitly identified as exceeding the engineering layer's reach, which is the only honest treatment. The chapter-closing function lands. Per the prompt's special focus on this verse: the operational scoping IS honest, the STRETCHED tag IS correctly applied, the chapter-thesis-support persona explicitly identifies 2.72 as the chapter's closing point — all three checks the prompt called for are present in the verse-record. Pass on both 10.3 and 10.4.
